input_transcript: Alice: We need to finish the UI this week. Bob: I will handle the backend API. Carol: Let's meet again on Friday.
target_summary: The team discussed completing the UI this week, assigned the backend API task to Bob, and agreed to meet again on Friday.
target_action_items: Bob will handle the backend API.
The team will meet again on Friday.

input_transcript: David: The transcript quality was not very good in the first half. Emma: We should improve the audio pipeline before the next demo. Frank: I can test the recording settings tomorrow.
target_summary: The team noted poor transcript quality in the first half of the meeting and agreed to improve the audio pipeline before the next demo.
target_action_items: Frank will test the recording settings tomorrow.
The team will improve the audio pipeline before the next demo.

input_transcript: Grace: We need a clearer feedback flow for users after summary generation. Henry: Let us add a rating button and an edit box. Irene: We should store both the original summary and the edited version for retraining.
target_summary: The team discussed adding a clearer feedback flow after summary generation, including a rating button and an edit box, and decided to store both original and edited summaries for retraining.
target_action_items: Add a rating button to the feedback interface.
Add an edit box for summary revision.
Store both original and edited summaries for retraining.

input_transcript: Alice opened the project sync and said the team needs a stable demo by Friday. Bob reported that the ASR endpoint is now returning transcript text correctly for uploaded audio files. Carol said the summarization model still fails during inference in MLflow registry. The team agreed to stop changing multiple things at once and first create a working registered summarizer version. Bob will test the online pipeline with one audio file only. Carol will retrain the summarization model and register a new production alias. Alice will prepare the final demo script after the pipeline is stable.
target_summary: The team reviewed demo readiness for Friday. The ASR endpoint is working, but the summarization model still fails in MLflow registry. They agreed to focus on creating one stable registered summarizer version before making other changes. Bob will test the online pipeline on a single audio file, Carol will retrain and register the summarizer, and Alice will prepare the demo script once the pipeline is stable.

input_transcript: David started the meeting by asking why the retraining script could not find the training file. Emma explained that the config file still points to retraining_ready_train.jsonl, but that file has not been generated yet. Frank suggested using the sample retraining file first so the team can validate the training and model registration flow. Emma also noted that once the sample run works, the API based dataset generation can be added back. David agreed and asked everyone to avoid debugging the online pipeline until a new summarizer version is successfully registered.
target_summary: The team found that the training script failed because the config referenced a nonexistent retraining file. Emma recommended using the sample retraining file first to validate training and model registration before returning to the API based dataset flow. David agreed that the priority is to successfully register a new summarizer version before debugging the online pipeline further.

input_transcript: Grace reviewed the MLflow model registry and found that the production alias for jitsi-summarizer still points to version 6. Henry said this explains why the online pipeline keeps downloading a broken model even after the local train.py file was fixed. Irene proposed retraining with the corrected text2text-generation pipeline and then manually switching the production alias if needed. The team decided to confirm the new version number after training and then rerun the online pipeline using that version directly before testing the alias.
target_summary: The team confirmed that the production alias for jitsi-summarizer still points to the broken version 6, which is why the online pipeline continues to fail. They decided to retrain the summarizer using the corrected text2text-generation pipeline, verify the new version number, and test the online pipeline against that version directly before relying on the production alias.

input_transcript: Jack said the training dataset currently contains only three examples, which is too small for the existing split settings. Kelly explained that validation_split combined with make_test_from_validation caused the script to split one validation example again, leading to an empty train set error. Leo suggested disabling test generation from validation for now and lowering the quality gate threshold to zero so the model can still be registered for integration testing. Everyone agreed that these relaxed settings are acceptable for the demo stage.
target_summary: The meeting focused on a training failure caused by an extremely small dataset. The team determined that the split settings were creating an empty training set. They agreed to disable test generation from the validation split and temporarily lower the quality gate threshold to zero so the summarizer can still be trained and registered for integration testing.

input_transcript: Mia summarized the API situation. She said the backend provides GET endpoints for transcripts, summaries, reviews by meeting, and individual meeting lookup, but there is no endpoint that lists all meetings. Noah said that because of this limitation, the retraining pipeline cannot automatically discover every meeting from the API alone. Olivia proposed keeping a local meeting manifest file that stores each meeting_id when the online pipeline creates a meeting. The team agreed that the manifest based approach is the fastest way to support retraining without changing the backend.
target_summary: The team reviewed available backend APIs and noted that there is no endpoint for listing all meetings. Because of this, the retraining workflow cannot discover meeting records automatically from the API alone. They decided to maintain a local meeting manifest file that records each meeting_id when the online pipeline creates a meeting, since this is the fastest solution without backend changes.

input_transcript: Paul opened the deployment check and asked whether the MinIO and MLflow endpoints were configured consistently across scripts. Quinn verified that the shared platform uses port 30500 for MLflow tracking and port 30900 for the S3 endpoint. Rachel found that the online pipeline and training script had already been updated with those values. The remaining issue was not endpoint configuration but the stale summarizer model stored in the registry. Paul concluded that environment setup is mostly correct and the next milestone is to publish a clean summarizer artifact.
target_summary: The team confirmed that the shared MLflow and MinIO endpoints are configured correctly in the scripts. They concluded that the remaining failure is not caused by endpoint settings but by a stale summarizer model in the registry. The next priority is to publish a clean summarizer artifact.

input_transcript: Sophia reviewed the expected demo flow. A user uploads or selects an audio file from MinIO, the pipeline creates a meeting record, downloads the audio, runs ASR, uploads the transcript text, stores the transcript through the API, reads the transcript back, runs summarization, stores the summary, and finally creates a review record. Thomas said that the demo only needs one file to work end to end. Sophia emphasized that stability matters more than model quality for the class demonstration. The team agreed to keep the pipeline simple and avoid optional features such as action item extraction for now.
target_summary: The team reviewed the full demo workflow from audio retrieval through ASR, transcript storage, summarization, summary storage, and review creation. They agreed that only one file needs to run end to end for the demo and that stability is more important than model quality. Optional features like action item extraction will be postponed.

input_transcript: Uma asked how review data should be converted into retraining samples. Victor answered that the edited summary should become the target summary, while the meeting transcript should become the model input. Wendy added that ratings and approval flags can be used as filters so only high quality feedback is included. The group decided that approved reviews with strong ratings are enough for the first retraining dataset. They also agreed to keep extra fields like reviewer_id and correction_label in the JSONL file for traceability, even if the model training only uses the transcript and summary columns.
target_summary: The team defined how review data should be converted into retraining samples. The edited summary will be used as the target output and the meeting transcript as the model input. They decided to include only approved, highly rated reviews in the first retraining dataset and to preserve extra metadata fields for traceability even though training only uses the transcript and summary columns.

input_transcript: Xavier checked the model training configuration and said the output directory, learning rate, and batch size are fine for a minimal run. Yvonne warned that the evaluation threshold should not block registration during integration testing. Zach recommended setting the quality gate threshold to zero temporarily and restoring a stricter value later. Xavier also said that once a new version appears in the registry, the team should test the online pipeline by explicitly referencing that version number before switching back to the production alias. Everyone agreed with the plan.
target_summary: The team reviewed the training configuration and found it acceptable for a minimal run. They agreed to temporarily set the quality gate threshold to zero so registration is not blocked during integration testing. Once a new model version is created, they will test the online pipeline against that explicit version before relying on the production alias again.

input_transcript: Amy led a short troubleshooting session on why the online pipeline still reported unknown task summarization. Brian explained that changing the local train.py file does not affect the already registered model artifact in MLflow. Chloe added that the registry entry must be replaced by training and registering a new version from the corrected code. Amy concluded that the team should separate local code fixes from registry state and verify both every time. Brian will check the alias target after the next training run, and Chloe will rerun the online pipeline only after the alias is updated.
target_summary: The team clarified that fixing the local training code does not change the model artifact already stored in MLflow. To resolve the unknown task error, they need to train and register a new summarizer version from the corrected code. They agreed to verify both the local code and the registry alias after each training run, and to rerun the online pipeline only after the alias is updated.

input_transcript: Ben opened the meeting by asking how the team can reduce confusion during debugging. Clara suggested testing one component at a time: first model training, then registry aliasing, then the online pipeline, and finally retraining data generation. Daniel said that when multiple failures appear at once, the team should record which step passed and which step failed. Clara proposed keeping short terminal logs for each stage so that the same mistakes are not repeated. Ben agreed and asked everyone to use a more disciplined step by step validation process until the demo is complete.
target_summary: The team discussed how to debug more effectively. They agreed to validate one component at a time, starting with model training, then registry aliasing, then the online pipeline, and finally retraining data generation. They also decided to keep short logs for each stage and clearly record which steps pass or fail to avoid repeated confusion.

input_transcript: Diana reviewed the final integration goal. The system should read audio from MinIO, produce a transcript with the ASR model, summarize the transcript with the summarization model, store both outputs through the API, and later reuse approved review data for retraining. Ethan said the architecture is acceptable for the class project as long as every handoff is clear and reproducible. Fiona added that automated retraining can happen after the demo if time is limited. Diana concluded that the immediate priority is to make the main inference path reliable and use simple sample data to publish a working summarizer model version.
target_summary: The team restated the final integration goal: process audio from MinIO, generate a transcript with ASR, summarize it, store the outputs through the API, and later use approved reviews for retraining. They agreed that the immediate priority is to make the main inference path reliable and to use simple sample data now so they can publish a working summarizer model version.
